aliases: Ham and cheese on wholemeal bread

english: Ham and cheese on wholemeal bread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for ham and cheese sandwich on wholemeal bread; 'sandwich food product' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers sandwiches as a category; no specific wholemeal ham and cheese sandwich code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for ham and cheese sandwich on wholemeal bread.

serving.description: 1 sandwich (approximately 2 slices wholemeal bread with ham and cheese filling)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one whole sandwich made with two slices of wholemeal bread, approximately 150 g, providing around 569 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Ham and Cheese Sandwich (Wholemeal) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ham and Cheese Sandwich (Wholemeal), cr_id F05020053, with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite (ham + cheddar + wholemeal bread) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wholemeal bread and sandwich composite estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a ham and cheese sandwich on wholemeal bread in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated using wholemeal bread GI (approximately 69–71, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward to approximately 65 to account for the protein and fat content of ham and cheese, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (65 × 20.31 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 19.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (502 mg/100 g) from processed ham and cheese, with very low potassium (31 mg/100 g) providing negligible counterbalance
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this sandwich due to its high sodium load of 502 mg per 100 g (approximately 753 mg per 150 g serving); if consumed, choose reduced-sodium ham and cheese and limit to half a sandwich.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (20.3 g/100 g) with medium GI (65) and high fat (28.5 g/100 g) including estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) that may worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this sandwich to half a serving, choose wholemeal over white bread for its higher fibre content (2.5 g/100 g), avoid margarine-based spreads to eliminate trans fat, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the medium glycemic index of 65.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (28.5 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~9.8 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and high sodium (502 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this sandwich sparingly; the combination of high saturated fat, estimated trans fat from margarine, and high sodium significantly increases LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk, and substituting avocado or olive oil spread for margarine and using low-sodium ham would meaningfully reduce the cardiovascular burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from margarine and arachidonic acid from processed pork ham are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this sandwich in its standard form due to pro-inflammatory trans fat from margarine and arachidonic acid from processed ham; replacing margarine with extra-virgin olive oil spread and substituting smoked salmon for ham would substantially reduce the inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham and Cheese Sandwich (Wholemeal) is a Western-style filled sandwich consisting of two slices of wholemeal bread enclosing sliced processed ham and sliced cheese, typically assembled with a margarine or butter spread. The wholemeal bread provides a denser, slightly nutty texture and a higher dietary fibre content (2.49 g/100 g) compared to white bread, making it a marginally better choice for blood glucose management. The sandwich is energy-dense at 379 kcal per 100 g, driven primarily by its high fat content of 28.47 g/100 g, which includes an estimated 9.8 g/100 g saturated fat from cheese and ham and approximately 0.2 g/100 g trans fat from margarine. Protein content is moderate at 10.94 g/100 g, contributed by both ham and cheese. Sodium is high at 502 mg/100 g, reflecting the processed nature of the ham and cheese components. Calcium is notably high at 364 mg/100 g, primarily from the cheese. The sandwich carries a medium glycemic index of approximately 65, moderated by the protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or school canteen meal in Singapore and across Western contexts. Clinically, this food is of concern for individuals with hypertension (high sodium, low potassium), cardiovascular disease (high saturated and trans fat, high sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory trans fat and arachidonic acid from pork). For Type 2 Diabetes, the wholemeal variant is preferable to white bread but portion control and avoidance of margarine remain important. It is not suitable for halal diets due to pork ham content, and contains gluten and dairy allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020053, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database wholemeal bread anchor with documented adjustment rationale.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite sandwich is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F05020053) and was not available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact preparation as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported by HPB for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond calcium, iron, potassium, and cholesterol is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. saturated_fat_g (9.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite of wholemeal bread, processed ham, and cheddar cheese in proportions consistent with the HPB description; this is an adapted estimate and not directly reported by HPB. trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) is an estimate based on margarine content typical of this preparation style; not directly measured in the HPB source. potassium_mg value of 31.0 mg/100 g is notably low and is preserved as reported by HPB (F05020053); this may reflect the specific preparation or a data limitation in the source. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate anchored to wholemeal bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for the protein and fat matrix of the sandwich filling. This food is not suitable for halal diets due to pork ham. Allergens: gluten (wholemeal bread), dairy (cheese, margarine).
